coredata.dc:description: Food spoilage caused by bacteria led to about 600 million people suffering from foodborne diseases in 2020, and the diseases kill approximately 400,000 people annually. Traditional food preservatives, antibiotics, and nanomaterials have problems such as high dosage, bacterial resistance, and low biocompatibility. In this study, mild antibacterial carbon dots (TM-CDs) with Fe3+ nutritional competitiveness were prepared. TM-CDs specifically chelated Fe3+ and formed Fe-O. The maximum chelating capacity of TM-CDs for Fe3+ was 154.56 mg mL−1. TM-CDs exhibited a much stronger chelating ability for Fe3+ than traditional iron chelators and even siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi. The minimum inhibitory concentration of TM-CDs against Pseudomonas fragi was 0.25 mg mL−1. The antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was the existence of Fe3+ competition between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi, which ultimately led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi due to iron deficiency. Notably, no obvious cytotoxicity of TM CDs was observed. In addition, the experimental results of freshness showed that TM-CDs effectively prolonged the shelf-life of salmon by 3–4 d. Therefore, this study provides insight into developing a competitive approach to depriving bacteria of nutrients for antibacterial purposes, with potential applications in seafood storage and preservation.

head.ce:title: Super Fe3+ competing ability, high biocompatibility, and mild antibacterial carbon dots for food preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food spoilage caused by bacteria led to about 600 million people suffering from foodborne diseases in 2020, and the diseases kill approximately 400,000 people annually. Traditional food preservatives, antibiotics, and nanomaterials have problems such as high dosage, bacterial resistance, and low biocompatibility. In this study, mild antibacterial carbon dots (TM-CDs) with Fe3+ nutritional competitiveness were prepared. TM-CDs specifically chelated Fe3+ and formed Fe-O. The maximum chelating capacity of TM-CDs for Fe3+ was 154.56 mg mL−1. TM-CDs exhibited a much stronger chelating ability for Fe3+ than traditional iron chelators and even siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi. The minimum inhibitory concentration of TM-CDs against Pseudomonas fragi was 0.25 mg mL−1. The antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was the existence of Fe3+ competition between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi, which ultimately led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi due to iron deficiency. Notably, no obvious cytotoxicity of TM CDs was observed. In addition, the experimental results of freshness showed that TM-CDs effectively prolonged the shelf-life of salmon by 3–4 d. Therefore, this study provides insight into developing a competitive approach to depriving bacteria of nutrients for antibacterial purposes, with potential applications in seafood storage and preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Seafood is attracted to people because of its rich nutrients and unique flavor. However, seafood, like other perishable food, is susceptible to spoilage caused by foodborne bacteria. The World Health Organization report showed that foodborne diseases infected approximately 600 million people in 2020, and approximately 400,000 people died annually [1,2]. Bacteria are the main pathogenic factor causing foodborne diseases. People usually contract foodborne diseases when they consume food that is contaminated by pathogenic bacteria and spoilage bacteria. Food provides pathogenic bacteria and spoilage bacteria with nutrients, thereby enhancing their growth. Iron is one of the indispensable nutrients for the life-sustaining activities of bacteria. Bacteria generally need 10-7–10-5 M of bioavailable iron to achieve optimal growth [3]. As a trace element in food, iron is rarely used directly by bacteria. As a result, most foodborne bacteria will generate iron-chelating molecules, called siderophores, for iron absorption. When the iron content in the environment where bacteria live gradually decreases, the amount of siderophore secreted by bacteria will increase accordingly. Bacteria acquire Fe3+ from the environment by secreting siderophores. Consequently, bacteria that cannot secrete siderophores and cannot use siderophores produced by other microorganisms will die due to iron deficiency. This iron competition represents one of the most important mechanisms of nutritional competition among the bacteria. The nutrient competition mechanism of siderophores widely exists in the biological community. For example, cyanobacteria [4], Poaceae [5], probiotics [6], Pseudomonas fluorescens strains [7,8], Trichoderma [9], rhizosphere bacteria [10,11], and Bacillus [12–14] can compete for Fe3+ in the matrix by secreting siderophores to produce antibacterial effects, effectively inhibiting the growth of other microorganisms. Spoilage bacteria become dominant during the spoilage of aquatic products by secreting siderophores to compete for Fe3+ in the matrix, thereby facilitating the spoilage of aquatic products. Therefore, the absorption of iron through siderophores is indispensable in most infections caused by foodborne bacteria, and siderophores have more affinity for Fe3+ than Fe2+. Currently, few methods are used to prevent bacteria from using siderophores to obtain iron from the host [15]. Thus, using Fe3+ nutrition competition to inhibit bacteria provides a new opinion for developing new mild bacteriostatic agents and aquatic product preservation technology.

To overcome the adverse negative effect of foodborne bacteria on public health, researchers have developed natural and synthetic food preservatives with antibacterial activity. However, these preservatives are used in large quantities, exhibit less bacteriostatic effect, do not have a mild action, and can easily destroy food components and nutrition [16]. In addition, some antibiotics are widely used in foodborne diseases, but their abuse intensifies the antibiotic resistance of bacteria [17,18]. The emergence of nanotechnology provides a new strategy for the design of non-antibiotic antibacterial agents. A variety of nano-antibacterial agents, including carbon nanomaterials, metal oxide nanomaterials, and noble metal nanomaterials, have replaced most traditional antibacterial agents. However, the application of these nano-antibacterial agents in food is affected by their high cost and inherent cytotoxicity [19,20]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop non-toxic, efficient, and environment-friendly antibacterial substances. Carbon dots are a new type of nanomaterials and are known for good photoelectric properties, biocompatibility, stability, low toxicity, and fluorescence, making them promising materials for various applications, especially in the development of antibacterial properties in recent years. The antibacterial mechanisms of carbon dots include physical destruction of bacterial surface, generation of ROS (reactive oxygen species) to destroy bacterial structure, interference with the structure of bacterial DNA and RNA, coupling with antibiotics, destruction of bacterial peptidoglycan biosynthesis, and induction of bacterial programmed death. At present, carbon dots, as a new type of antibacterial agent, have been applied in the preservation of fruits, vegetables, meat, and other foods [21].

In this study, a type of carbon dots (TM-CDs) capable of chelating Fe3+, which is a mild antibacterial method, was developed. TM-CDs can inhibit the growth of bacteria by blocking their absorption of nutrients (iron). The new antibacterial method was applied to preserve salmon rich in iron. Pseudomonas fragi, the dominant spoilage bacterium of salmon [22,23], was the object of this study. It is known for its ability to produce siderophores. The successful synthesis of TM-CDs was verified using various characterization methods, and the mechanism and ability of TM-CDs to chelate Fe3+ were studied. The result showed that TM-CDs had stronger chelating ability to Fe3+ than siderophores and then inhibited the growth of Pseudomonas fragi by capturing Fe3+. In addition, the preservation ability of TM-CDs was evaluated. The sensory quality and seafood spoilage indexes [total viable count (TVC), thiobarbituric acid (TBA), total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), water-holding capacity (WHC), and K value] of salmon treated with sodium dehydroacetate (a traditional food preservative) and TM-CDs during storage at 4℃ were determined. In conclusion, this study aimed to design a new antibacterial method: the existence of Fe3+ competition between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi, which ultimately led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi due to iron deficiency and applies it to the preservation of seafood (Scheme 1 ).

L-Theanine (AR, 99 %) and DL-Malic acid (AR, ＞ 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corp (Shanghai, China). Salmon was purchased from the Jinzhou seafood market, Liaoning Province, China, and originated in Norway. Pseudomonas fragi comes from the laboratory of Bohai University, Jinzhou City, Liaoning Province, China.

Add 1.2 g L-Theanine and 0.8 g DL-Malic acid into a 100 mL conical flask, then add 10 mL deionized water to it, mix well, and treat it with microwave at 800 w in a microwave oven for 3 min to obtain a black–brown mucus liquid, and then add 10 mL deionized water to it to completely dissolve. The impurities in the solution were filtered out, and then the filtered solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 12 h. Finally, TM-CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying for further characterization and application.

The microscopic morphology, size, and crystal structure of TM-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The morphology of TM-CDs was observed by atomic force microscope (AFM). Raman spectra of TM-CDs were measured by a Raman spectrometer. The UV–vis spectra were determined using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of TM-CDs were determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined by an X-ray diffractometer in the scanning range of 10°–80°. The infrared spectra and main functional groups of TM-CDs were obtained by a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. The main element composition of TM-CDs was obtained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para:
CCK-8 assay was used to determine whether TM-CDs could inhibit the proliferation of MC3T3-E1 cells [24]. MC3T3-E1 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate, and the number of cells in each well was 5 × 103. The cells were cultured overnight in an incubator with 37℃ and 5 % CO2. Cell culture medium (90 % α-MEM culture medium + 10 % fetal bovine serum) and TM-CDs with different concentrations (0–1 mg mL−1) were added to each well in turn, and then the culture was continued for 24 h. Add 10 μ L CCK-8 solution for staining, and then incubated in the incubator for 1 h. Observe the OD450 nm (OD value at 450 nm) of each well and calculate the cell proliferation rate according to the following Eq. (1).
(1) Cell viability (%) = Abs(a)/Abs(b) × 100 %
where, Abs(a) and Abs(b) are the absorbance of the sample and absorbance of control, respectively.

In this experiment, 4 % rat red blood cells were selected. The red blood cells were mixed with TM-CDs with a concentration of 0.25–1.5 mg mL−1. After incubation in an incubator at 37℃ for 3 h and centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min. Hemolysis was determined by observing the OD576 nm (hemoglobin) of the supernatant. PBS was used as a 0 % hemolysis reference (OD576 nm blank). Ultrapure water was used as a 100 % hemolysis reference (OD576 nm ultrapure water) [25]. Hemolytic activity was calculated according to the following Eq. (2):
(2) Hemolysis (%) = (OD576 nm TM-CDs-OD576 nm blank)/ (OD576 nm ultrapure water-OD576 nm blank)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific selection of TM-CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: TM-CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) were mixed with Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, and Fe3+ with a concentration of 6 mM in the same volume. TM-CDs with the same volume was added after other metal ions were mixed with Fe3+ in pairs. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Using fluorescence quenching rate F/F0 evaluated the fluorescence quenching degree of TM-CDs caused by metal ions, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of each solution (in the presence and absence of metal ions) [26].

Added TM-CDs with the concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 to Fe3+ solutions with initial concentrations of 5, 10, 15, 30, 50, 70, and 100 mg L-1 respectively, and after fully mixing for 6 h, measured the equilibrium concentration after the reaction by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), and calculated the chelating performance of TM-CDs to Fe3+ by using the following Eq. (3):
(3) qe = (C0-Ce) × V/M

where, qe is the adsorption capacity (mg g−1) by the adsorbent, C0 is the initial concentration (mg L-1), Ce is the equilibrium concentration (mg L-1), V is the volume of solution (L), and M is the mass of adsorbent (g) used [27].

Using the nonlinear forms of Langmuir (Eq. (4)) and Freundlich (Eq. (5)) described the equilibrium data, respectively:
(4) qe = qmaxKLCe/(1 + KLCe)
(5) qe = KFCe 1/n

where, qe (mg g−1) and qmax (mg g−1) are respectively the equilibrium adsorption capacity of TM-CDs for Fe3+ and the theoretical maximum equilibrium adsorption capacity, Ce (mg L-1) is the equilibrium concentration of Fe3+, KL (L mg−1) refers to Langmuir constant, KF refers to Freundlich constant, and 1/n is the empirical constant that presents the adsorption strength [28].

The MIC of TM-CDs against Pseudomonas fragi was tested. Pseudomonas fragi was cultured overnight to the logarithmic growth phase. Used PBS to dilute Pseudomonas fragi to 6 log CFU mL−1. The suspension of Pseudomonas fragi was centrifuged to remove the supernatant, and an equal volume of the LB broth medium was added to it. TM-CDs, L-Theanine, and DL-Malic acid were mixed with the above LB broth medium in equal volume for 12 h and then spread on the LB nutrient agar to observe the growth of colonies. The concentrations of TM-CDs, L-Theanine, and L-Malic acid were all 0.0625–0.25 mg mL−1. In addition, the plate containing only Pseudomonas fragi was used as a positive control, and the plate containing Pseudomonas fragi and L-Theanine or L-Malic acid was used as a negative control.

Determination of the activity of Pseudomonas fragi after adding TM-CDs. The experimental steps were like the MIC experiment. TM-CDs (0.125, 0.25, 0.5 mg mL−1) were co-cultured with Pseudomonas fragi in the LB broth medium for 12 h, and then the mixed solution was washed with PBS. 1.5 µL of SYTO-9 and 1.5 µL of PI were added to 1 mL of Pseudomonas fragi suspension, and after 15 min of reaction, the activity of the bacteria was observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) [29].

Pseudomonas fragi was cultured overnight to the logarithmic growth phase and diluted to 6 log CFU mL−1. Prepared 4 tubes of fresh LB broth medium, and number them as (1), (2), (3), and (4). Among them, (1) only Pseudomonas fragi; (2) containing Pseudomonas fragi and Fe3+; (3) containing Pseudomonas fragi and TM-CDs; (4) contains Pseudomonas fragi, TM-CDs, and Fe3+. The inoculum size of Pseudomonas fragi is consistent, the concentration of TM-CDs is MIC, and the concentration of Fe3+ is 2 µM (this concentration has no toxic effect on Pseudomonas fragi). After the four tubes of LB broth medium were cultured at 28℃ and 160 r/min for 12 h, the difference of OD600 nm among the four groups was observed, and they were spread on the LB nutrient agar to observe the difference of colonies. According to the above experimental steps, the growth curves of four groups of experiments were explored. The concentration of Pseudomonas fragi was 6 log CFU mL−1, and OD600 nm was recorded every 4 h.

Pseudomonas fragi was inoculated into MKB liquid medium and cultured at 28℃ and 160 r/min to logarithmic growth phase. The fermentation broth was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and a filter membrane (0.22 µm) was used to obtain sterile supernatant. The supernatant was mixed with XAD-2 macroporous resin and adsorbed at 20℃ and 100 r/min overnight. The XAD-2 macroporous resin was separated from the supernatant, and an appropriate volume of 75 % methanol/water was added, which was put into a chromatography column. At a flow rate of 1.5 mL min−1, elute twice the column volume with 75 % methanol/water. After the elution, detected the collected components with Chrome azurol S (CAS) detection solution, evaporated the useful components in a rotary evaporator at 40℃ and 80 r/min, and then added sterile deionized water to dissolve the samples. Added the sample solution to Sephadex LH-20 for further purification. The eluent is 10 % methanol/water (1.5 mL min−1). After the elution, the collected samples of each component were detected by CAS detection solution, and the useful components were freeze-dried to obtain viscous solid, that was, the separated and purified siderophores. Siderophores in fermentation broth, siderophores separated by XAD-2 macroporous resin, and siderophores separated by XAD-2 macroporous resin and purified by Sephadex LH-20 were respectively dropped into CAS agar diffusion (CASAD) detection plate respectively, and the size of the yellow circle was observed and the relative content of siderophores was determined by CAS detection solution [30,31].

Equal amounts of TM-CDs and purified siderophores were prepared. The TM-CDs, siderophores, and TM-CDs–siderophores mixtures were added into the Fe3+ solution (5 mg L-1) respectively, and the adsorption kinetics was studied at 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, and 6 h, and the chelating processes of the three were compared. 5 mg L-1 meets the iron concentration required for the optimal growth of bacteria. Among them, using the pseudo-first-order kinetics model and pseudo-second-order kinetics model explained the kinetics of Fe3+ adsorption by the three. Eq. (6) and (7) are described as follows:
(6) qt = qe (1-e-K1t)
(7) qt = qe 2 K2 t/(1 + qe K2t)

where, K1 (min−1) is a pseudo-first-order rate constant, K2(g·(mg·min)-1) is a pseudo-second-order rate constant, t (min) is the contact time, and qe (mg g−1) and qt (mg g−1) are the adsorption capacities of Fe3+ at equilibrium and at different time t, respectively [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TM-CDs in salmon storage
ce:section.ce:para: Cut the salmon into 2 cm × 2 cm × 2 cm fillets. Soaked the fillets in sterile distilled water, sodium dehydroacetate solution and TM-CDs solution (the concentration of sodium dehydroacetate is consistent with the MIC of TM-CDs) for 10 min. The samples were put in a fresh-keeping box and stored in a refrigerator at 4℃ for 12 d, and the quality changes of fish fillets (TVC, TBA, TVB-N, WHC, and K value) were observed every 3 days. The salmon samples were stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) Staining Kit on days 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 [33]. The experimental steps are provided in the supporting information.
All experimental data were tested in three parallel groups. All experimental data were analyzed by Origin 2021 and SPSS 26.0 software, and a P-value < 0.05 was considered as significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, characterization, and toxicity analysis of TM-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we employed L-Theanine and DL-Malic acid as raw materials, successfully synthesizing TM-CDs through microwave heating for 3 min. The carbon dots are characterized by low cost (the cost of producing 1 g of carbon dots is RMB 2), high yield (67.5 %, 2 g of raw material can yield 1.35 g of TM-CDs), rapid synthesis (3 min), and green and non-toxic. The microstructure of TM-CDs was characterized by TEM. TM-CDs had good dispersibility and presented a spherical state (Fig. 1 a). The inset of Fig. 1a shows 3.50 ± 1.55 nm (n = 48) as the average diameter of the particles. The HRTEM image of TM-CDs showed that the lattice spacing was about 0.26 nm (Fig. 1a inset), which belonged to the (100) lattice plane of graphite [33]. AFM images showed clear spherical particles, which was consistent with TEM results [34,35] (Fig. 1b). Specific statistics are shown in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 1c, Raman spectra of TM-CDs showed two peaks near 1376.70 and 1574.74 cm−1, which belonged to D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2) respectively, and the ratio of ID/IG was 0.93. The above results proved that TM-CDs showed disordered carbon structure [36]. As shown in Fig. 1d, TM-CDs showed an ultraviolet absorption peak at 280 nm, which was attributed to π-π* transition of C = C bond [37]. The optimal emission and excitation peaks were located at 440 and 350 nm, respectively. Fig. 1d inset shows the optical images of deionized water and TM-CDs under UV light irradiation, in which the TM-CDs solution shows bright blue light. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, which indicated that TM-CDs could excite the maximum fluorescence emission intensity at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The wavelength shift behavior might be related to the different sizes of TM-CDs [38]. Fig. 1e shows the XRD patterns of TM-CDs. The center position of the crystalline planes (002) of graphite carbon was about 2θ = 20.3°. The result showed that TM-CDs formed a certain crystal structure but mainly existed in the form of disordered carbon nanoparticles, which indicated that TM-CDs had good surface activity [39]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the main functional groups of TM-CDs were determined by FT-IR spectrometry. The absorption peaks at 3084, 1542, and 1195 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H, C = O, and C-N, respectively [40]. The absorption peak at 1376 cm−1 was attributed to the in-plane deformation vibration of O-H [41]. These results showed that TM-CDs exhibited oxygen-enriched characteristics, excellent stability, and hydrophilicity because of the existence of these functional groups [42]. The XPS spectrum of TM-CDs showed that the peaks at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV were caused by C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1g). The results showed that TM-CDs mainly comprised carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, with atomic percentages of 61.16, 10.39, and 28.45 %, respectively. C1s spectrum showed revealed C-O, C = O, C-C, and C-N/C-NH2 in TM-CDs. These results showed that the surface of TM-CDs was rich in hydrophilic groups (Fig. S2) [43]. N1s spectrum revealed the formation of amide groups in TM-CDs (Fig. S3). O1s spectrum showed that TM-CDs contained C-O, C = O, and O-H (Fig. S4) [44]. The composition of functional groups of TM-CDs was analyzed by FT-IR and XPS, and the results were consistent. The CCK-8 assay showed that TM-CDs had no significant effect on the proliferation of MC3T3-E1 cells in the concentration range of 0.25–1.00 mg mL−1 (Fig. 1h). The hemolysis assay showed that TM-CDs did not rupture rat red blood cells in the concentration range of 0.25–1.50 mg mL−1 (Fig. 1i). Both experiments confirmed that TM-CDs exhibited the characteristics of safety and good biocompatibility.

The fluorescence stability of carbon dots plays a key role in fluorescence experiments. As shown in Figs. S5 and S6, TM-CDs exhibited good fluorescence stability at pH 3–10 and a storage time of 0–7 d [45]. The TM-CDs solution was added to seven kinds of metal ion solutions at a concentration of 6 mM for the fluorescence quenching experiment. The fluorescence quenching efficiency for TM-CDs by Fe3+ was the strongest (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2b, TM-CDs still maintained the fluorescence quenching behavior for Fe3+ in the presence of other metal ions. Therefore, the above experiments showed that TM-CDs had the characteristics of specific selection for Fe3+ [46]. Fig. 2c shows the emission spectra of TM-CDs solution mixed with Fe3+ solution with a 0–51.2 mM concentration. The fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase in Fe3+ concentration, confirming the existence of many Fe3+ binding sites of TM-CDs, and the 51.2 mM of Fe3+ made TM-CDs completely lose the fluorescence property [47]. HAADF-STEM image of TM-CDs showed that TM-CDs was composed of C, N, and O elements, which was consistent with XPS results (Fig. 2d). Fig. 2e shows HAADF-STEM image of TM-CDs + Fe3+ and Fig. 2f–i show the elemental mapping images. By comparing Fig. 2d and 2e, it could be found that Fe was uniformly distributed on the surface of TM-CDs, which proved that TM-CDs could chelate Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and ability of TM-CDs chelating Fe3+

ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a, the changes in functional groups after Fe3+ was added into TM-CDs were measured by FT-IR. C = O at 1542 cm−1 was red-shifted to 1595 cm−1; O-H at 1376 cm−1 was red-shifted to 1408 cm−1. Notably, Fe-O was at 595 cm−1 [48]. The above experimental results showed that oxygen-containing groups in TM-CDs formed coordination bonds with Fe3+. The specific principle was that O atoms on the surface of TM-CDs provide a lone pair of electrons to Fe3+, which leads to the formation of coordinate bonds between Fe3+ and O atoms [46,49,50]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the negative zeta potential (−7.43 mV) indicated that the surface of TM-CDs was rich in hydroxyl and carboxylate groups [51]. With the addition of Fe3+, the zeta potential of TM-CDs changed from −7.43 to 28.40 mV (Fig. 3b), which proved that hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of TM-CDs interacted with Fe3+ [52,53], which was consistent with the results of FT-IR spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the inhibition mechanism of TM-CDs on Pseudomonas fragi
ce:section.ce:para: TM-CDs, L-Theanine, and DL-Malic acid with a concentration of 0.0625–0.25 mg mL−1 were co-cultured with diluted Pseudomonas fragi for 12 h and then spread on LB nutrient agar, and the colony distribution was observed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the raw materials for synthesizing carbon dots (L-Theanine and DL-Malic acid) did not affect the normal growth of Pseudomonas fragi at the concentrations of 0.0625, 0.125, and 0.25 mg mL−1, while TM-CDs had no colony growth at 0.25 mg mL−1, and some colonies grew at 0.125 mg mL−1. Therefore, the MIC of TM-CDs against Pseudomonas fragi was 0.25 mg mL−1. The MIC of TM-CDs is approximately 10 times higher than that of the raw materials of TM-CDs (Fig. S7). The inhibitory mechanism of TM-CDs on Pseudomonas fragi might be that TM-CDs chelated iron in medium, which led to the failure of Pseudomonas fragi to absorb Fe3+ normally and die. The effects of different concentrations of TM-CDs on the activity of Pseudomonas fragi were observed using green SYTO 9 and red PI fluorescent stains. As shown in Fig. 4b, the green fluorescence represents the living Pseudomonas fragi, and the red fluorescence represents the dead Pseudomonas fragi. A small part of Pseudomonas fragi died at 0.5 × MIC, while a small amount of Pseudomonas fragi survived at 1 × MIC. No viable Pseudomonas fragi was observed at 2 × MIC. This result is consistent with that obtained through the plate count method [29]. Fig. 4c shows the effects of TM-CDs on the growth of Pseudomonas fragi at 2 × MIC, 1 × MIC, 0.5 × MIC, and 0.25 × MIC (the four concentrations of TM-CDs all affected the growth of Pseudomonas fragi). Among them, TM-CDs of 0.25 × MIC and 0.5 × MIC slowed down the growth of Pseudomonas fragi, while TM-CDs of 1 × MIC and 2 × MIC directly inhibited the growth of Pseudomonas fragi [57]. The reason for the above phenomenon might be that with the increase in the TM-CDs concentration, TM-CDs chelated more iron in the medium, which made Pseudomonas fragi use less iron and ultimately affected its growth.

To verify the above conjecture, Fe3+ was added to the medium to show the antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs more macroscopically. Pseudomonas fragi with the same concentration was inoculated into the medium, and Fe3+ (2 µM) with different volumes and the same concentration was added. After they were co-cultured for 8 h, the OD600 nm was determined. As shown in Fig. S8, when 100 µL of Fe3+ was added to the medium, the growth of Pseudomonas fragi was more pronounced, while when 150 µL and 200 µL of Fe3+ were added, the growth of Pseudomonas fragi was worse. Thus, Fe3+ with a concentration of 2 µM and a volume of 100 µL was selected for subsequent experiments. Fig. 4d shows the OD600 nm of four groups of experiments after 12 h of culture. The experiments were divided into four groups: (1) only Pseudomonas fragi; (2) containing Pseudomonas fragi and Fe3+; (3) containing Pseudomonas fragi and TM-CDs; (4) containing Pseudomonas fragi, TM-CDs, and Fe3+. From the comparison of (1) and (2), the growth of Pseudomonas fragi was strongly dependent on Fe3+. (1) and (3) and (2) and (4) all indicated that TM-CDs inhibited the growth of Pseudomonas fragi by chelating Fe3+, rather than directly killing Pseudomonas fragi. This antibacterial mechanism was consistent with that of carbon dot–hydrogel synthesized by Mahshid Chekini et al [56]. The inset of Fig. 4d shows that four groups of experiments are spread on LB nutrient agar. Fig. 4e shows the growth curves of four groups of experiments. Compared with (1), the growth of Pseudomonas fragi in (2) was faster and more pronounced. Pseudomonas fragi did not grow normally in both (3) and (4), but there was a slight growth trend, and the reasons for these phenomena were consistent with those in Fig. 4d. At present, the antibacterial mechanism of carbon dots is mainly to destroy the bacterial cell structure. To rule out that TM-CDs directly acts on Pseudomonas fragi, we conducted a cell membrane permeability test. When the fluorescence intensity becomes low, it indicates that the bacterial cell membrane is destroyed. As shown in Fig. 4f, TM-CDs did not destroy the cell membrane of Pseudomonas fragi compared with the control group, but tetracycline (a broad-spectrum antibacterial antibiotic) can destroy the cell membrane structure of Pseudomonas fragi, thus further indicating that TM-CDs does not directly act on Pseudomonas fragi, but there is nutritional competition of Fe3+. The above experiments confirmed that TM-CDs inhibited the growth of Pseudomonas fragi by chelating Fe3+. Pseudomonas fragi needs an iron supply in the process of growth and reproduction, and its main way of absorbing Fe3+ is by secreting siderophore. Therefore, it can be inferred that there is competition for Fe3+ between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi. To prove this conjecture, the siderophore secreted by Pseudomonas fragi was isolated, purified, and used in subsequent experiments.

The fermentation broth of Pseudomonas fragi cultured in MKB liquid medium was separated and purified using XAD-2 macroporous resin and Sephadex LH-20, and the purity of siderophores was evaluated using CAS detection solution [58] and CASAD detection plate [59]. As shown in Fig. 4g, Pseudomonas fragi produced more siderophores in the MKB liquid medium than in the LB broth medium because of the low iron content. The low iron content facilitated Pseudomonas fragi to secrete more siderophores to absorb iron to meet its own needs. The relative content of siderophores after separation and purification was 70.6 %. The CASAD detection plate was used for a more intuitive evaluation of the content of the siderophores after separation and purification. Fig. 4h shows the content of siderophores detected using CASAD detection plates. As shown in Fig. 4h inset, the diameters of the yellow circle produced by siderophores on CASAD detection plates were 8.77, 11.83, and 15.90 mm, respectively.

TM-CDs (0.1 mg mL−1), siderophores (0.143 mg mL−1, calculated from the relative content of siderophores after separation and purification), and TM-CDs–siderophores mixtures (0.1 mg mL−1:0.143 mg mL−1) were added to the 5 mg L-1 of Fe3+ solution. The adsorption kinetics was used to fit the process of chelating Fe3+ using the three. Fig. 4i shows the fitting results of the pseudo-first-order kinetics model and pseudo-second-order kinetics model of the three. The pseudo-first-order kinetics model fitted the data better (Table 2 ), indicating TM-CDs and siderophores were rich in chelating sites and could rapidly chelate to Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4i, TM-CDs were superior to siderophores in the rate and capacity of chelating Fe3+. The equilibrium chelating ability of TM-CD was 2.36 times that of siderophores when the initial concentration of Fe3+ solution was 5 mg L-1. (Table 2). TM-CDs–siderophores mixtures and TM-CDs almost maintained the same chelation process in the initial stage (Fig. 4i), and in the later stage, TM-CDs–siderophores mixtures chelated more Fe3+ than TM-CDs alone. Therefore, Fe3+ was preferentially chelated by TM-CDs when TM-CDs and siderophores coexist, and most of Fe3+ was chelated by TM-CDs, which proved that TM-CDs prevented Pseudomonas fragi from absorbing Fe3+ and eventually led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi.

The specific antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was as follows: It was confirmed by fluorescence quenching experiment, HAADF-STEM, FT-IR spectrum, and zeta potential that TM-CDs can specifically chelate Fe3+; The maximum chelating Fe3+ capacity of TM-CDs was 154.56 mg g−1 according to the adsorption isotherm; The competitive relationship between TM-CDs and siderophores on Fe3+ was established by adsorption kinetics, and the chelating ability of TM-CDs was stronger than that of siderophores. To sum up, the antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was the existence of Fe3+ competition between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi, which ultimately led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi due to iron deficiency (Fig. 4j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TM-CDs in salmon storage and preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The preservation performance of TM-CDs was evaluated by comparing the sensory quality, the microscopic state of muscle tissue, and the seafood preservation indexes (TVC, TBA, TVB-N, WHC, and K value) of untreated, sodium dehydroacetate (traditional food preservative) treated and TM-CDs treated salmon.

The change in the sensory quality of salmon directly reflected its edible value. The change in the quality of salmon was mainly due to the decomposition of protein in its body by bacteria. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the sensory quality of salmon showed a downward trend with the longer storage time. On day 0, all salmon samples were yellow–red and exhibited compact muscle, clear texture, and no odor. On the sixth day, the salmon in the control group turned dark–red, and the muscles began to loosen, with a putrid odor. The state of salmon in the sodium dehydroacetate group was slightly better, but there were also signs of spoilage. In contrast, the salmon of the TM-CDs group were in good condition. Its color remained yellow–red, and the muscle remained tightened and textured. On the ninth day, the salmon in the control group and the sodium dehydroacetate group continued to spoil, accompanied by oily substances on the surface, shriveled muscles, no elasticity, and a dark–red color. The salmon in the TM-CDs group began to show spoilage signs, and the surface began to appear dark–red and dull. After 12 days of storage, the salmon in the control group and the sodium dehydroacetate group were completely rotten and emitted a strong odor. However, the spoilage of salmon in the TM-CDs group was not as severe as that in the first two groups of samples. Therefore, TM-CDs can prolong the shelf-life of salmon for 3 days.

The microscopic state of salmon muscle tissue can directly reflect the quality and the degree of spoilage of salmon. As shown in Fig. 5b, on the 0th day, the muscle tissue fibers of salmon in the three groups were closely arranged together, showing a good fiber structure. On the sixth day, the salmon tissue fibers in the control group began to break, loosen, and wither. The main reason for this phenomenon is the decomposition of proteins by bacteria in salmon. A small part of muscle fibers in the sodium dehydroacetate group also decomposed. However, the salmon tissue fibers in the TM-CDs group were in good condition and tightly arranged, which effectively prevented the bacteria from growing and reproducing because of absorbed iron and then prevented them from decomposing protein. On the ninth day, the muscle fibers of salmon in the TM-CDs group broke. On the 12th day, the muscle fibers of the other two groups of fish except the TM-CDs group were broken, showing a chaotic state, which confirmed that the salmon was completely rotten. Conversely, some of the muscle fibers of salmon in the TM-CDs group remained intact, while some fibers were slack and broken. These results show that TM-CDs can effectively maintain the sensory quality of salmon.

Seafood can easily become a nutrient source for microorganisms because of its rich nutrients. TVC is an important index used to evaluate the extent of microbial contamination in food [60]. Generally, when the TVC value is greater than 6 log CFU g−1, it indicates that salmon has begun to rot and has no edible value. As shown in Fig. 5c, the TVC value of fresh salmon was 1.64 log CFU g−1. As the storage time increased, TVC values increased. In addition, the TVC value of salmon in the TM-CDs group was always lower than that in the other two groups, which indicated that TM-CDs had good antibacterial performance. On the sixth day, the TVC value of the control group was 6.14 ± 0.095 logCFU g−1, which indicated that the control group had begun to rot and the salmon in the sodium dehydroacetate group began to decay. In addition, the salmon in the TM-CDs group was in good condition, and there was no spoilage. On the ninth day, the TVC value of the salmon in the TM-CDs group was 5.96 ± 0.166 log CFU g−1, indicating that the salmon was about to enter the decay stage. The curve in the Fig. 5c shows that the salmon in the TM-CDs group began to decay on the 10th day. Therefore, TM-CDs can prolong the shelf-life of salmon by four days.

TBA value can reflect the degree of lipid oxidation in food and can also be used as a reference index of food freshness [61]. When bacteria pollute seafood, the growth and metabolism of bacteria will consume oxygen in meat, which will reduce the oxygen concentration on the surface of the meat, thus facilitating the oxidation reaction. Therefore, the spoilage of bacteria can indirectly lead to the oxidation of meat. Generally, when the TBA value exceeds 0.5 mg MDA kg−1, seafood will begin to decay and have no edible value. As the storage time extended, the TBA values of the three groups increased, and the TBA values of the TM-CDs group were lower than those of the other two groups (Fig. 5d). On the sixth day, the TBA value of the control group had exceeded 0.5 mg MDA kg−1, indicating that the control group began to decay. On the 10th day, the salmon in the TM-CDs group began to decay (Fig. 5d). Therefore, TM-CDs prolonged the shelf-life of salmon by four days.

TVB-N is a crucial index for evaluating the degree of spoilage of food [62]. TVB-N value can reflect the content of free amino compounds (such as amines) in fish meat, which are produced in the process of seafood spoilage. Generally, when the TVB-N value exceeds 30 mg (100 g)-1, seafood will decay. In the first six days, the TVB-N values of the three groups were lower than 30 mg (100 g)-1, but the control group was about to decay. The salmon in the control group deteriorated on the seventh day (Fig. 5e). On the 10th day, the salmon in the TM-CDs group began to decay. Therefore, TM-CDs prolonged the shelf-life of salmon by 3 days.

WHC is an important index to evaluate food quality [63]. Bacterial spoilage will lead to water loss of fish, making the surface of fish dry and stiff. As shown in Figure S9, the WHC values of three groups of salmon decreased. In the whole storage process, the WHC value of salmon in the TM-CDs group was the best, indicating that TM-CDs effectively inhibited the damage of bacteria to the fish meat and effectively retained the moisture content of salmon.

K value is an important indicator for evaluating the freshness of meat and fish products [64]. The K value is usually used to measure the adenosine triphosphate content in muscle tissue, thus reflecting the freshness and corruption of the product. Salmon is considered to enter the spoilage stage when its K value exceeds 35 %. On day 0, the K value of fresh salmon was 13.44 %. On the sixth day, the K value of the control group was 34.55 ± 1.24 %, indicating that the salmon was about to decay. In contrast, the K value of the TM-CDs group exceeded 35 % on the 10th day (Fig. S10).

In summary, the good application value of TM-CDs in the storage and preservation of salmon was confirmed by observing the sensory quality and the microscopic state of muscle tissue of salmon and measuring the preservation indicators of salmon (TVC, TBA, TVB-N, WHC, and K value). TM-CDs can prolong the shelf-life of salmon by 3–4 d. The main preservation mechanisms of TM-CDs were as follows: Iron in salmon is mainly distributed in heme and myoglobin. The total iron content in salmon is about 0.3–1.2 mg g−1, and the maximum chelating capacity of TM-CDs synthesized in this study is 154.56 mg g−1, while the time required for bacterial isolation and reproduction is 20–30 min. According to the results of the adsorption kinetic model, the chelating capacity of TM-CDs is 11.8 mg g−1 within 0.5 h (Fig. 4i). It is 4.57 times that of siderophores (2.58 mg g−1), so it can be inferred from the capacity and rate of chelating Fe3+ by TM-CDs that the bacteria in salmon died mainly because of the inability to effectively utilize iron. As non-toxic, green, and highly effective antibacterial carbon dots, TM CDs will play a good application value in the field of seafood preservation.

In this study, antibacterial TM-CDs with low cost, high yield, convenient synthesis, safety, and Fe3+ chelating ability was prepared by microwave heating. Fluorescence quenching test showed that TM-CDs could specifically chelate Fe3+. HAADF-STEM images showed that Fe could be uniformly distributed on the surface of TM-CDs. FT-IR spectrum and zeta potential proved the interaction between oxygen-containing groups on the surface of TM-CDs and Fe3+. The adsorption isotherm showed that the maximum chelating capacity of TM-CDs for Fe3+ was 154.56 mg g−1. The adsorption kinetic model showed that the rate and ability of TM-CDs chelating Fe3+ was much stronger than that of siderophores. According to this characteristic of TM-CDs chelating Fe3+, antibacterial experiments were designed, which proved that the antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was that the antibacterial mechanism of TM-CDs was the existence of Fe3+ competition between TM-CDs and siderophores secreted by Pseudomonas fragi, which ultimately led to the death of Pseudomonas fragi due to iron deficiency, and its minimum inhibitory concentration was 0.25 mg mL−1. By comparing the changes of sensory quality, muscle tissue, TVC, TBA, TVB-N, WHC, and K value of salmon, it was proved that antibacterial TM-CDs could effectively prolong the shelf-life of salmon by 3–4 days. Therefore, TM-CDs, as carbon dots with a new bacteriostatic method, can effectively meet the demand for seafood preservation. In addition, the unique antibacterial mode of TM-CDs may become a new trend.
